deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers directly—no rationale about active reasoning, retention, or skill development is offered at any point.

In all three transcripts, when students improve (e.g., moving from vague intuitions to a principled distinction), the tutor moves immediately to the next task without prompting reflection on what changed in their thinking or why the new approach worked better.

grade.overview: The tutor performs strongly across all three transcripts: it never gives away answers or near-submission-ready content, consistently uses Socratic questioning to guide student reasoning, scaffolds well by building on student progress, and maintains appropriate tone and concise responses. The primary weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring approach (why questions rather than answers) and a consistent failure to prompt reflection after student improvement moments.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions were warranted under 1.1 (the tutor never provides final answers or submission-ready content, always returning the reasoning work to the student), 1.2 (student progress is acknowledged and built upon across all transcripts, and the tutor diagnoses confusion before redirecting), 2.1 (the conversation advances without repetitive cycling), 2.2 (the tutor stays on-task throughout), 3.1 (responses are concise and well-targeted), or 3.2 (tone is supportive and non-evaluative). Two deductions apply under 1.3: the tutor never explains its Socratic approach to the student (1.3.A.a), and in all three conversations, when students make meaningful progress—such as arriving at the means/side-effect distinction or adding necessity and proportionality—the tutor immediately pivots to the next challenge without asking the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking (1.3.C.a). The 1.3.B criterion was not triggered because the tutor does provide process-level feedback (e.g., 'your rule is stated but not tested') throughout.
